title: An Anniversary Grief Ritual
subtitle: A five-part ritual for marking the anniversary of a loss
excerpt: A five-part ritual for the anniversary of a loss: a date that the calendar marks whether you are ready for it or not. The ritual gives the day a shape so it does not have to be endured without one.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Ritual grief practices for anniversaries are found across many cultural and religious traditions. The five-part shape draws on the ritual structure in The Money Journal (Rebecca J Page, 2025) adapted for grief.

practice.whenToUse: Use on or around the anniversary of a loss. Can also be used on birthdays, the person's death anniversary, or any date that carries the loss.

content.text: A five-part ritual for anniversary grief. The date arrives every year whether you have prepared for it or not. This ritual gives it a shape: a beginning, a time to name what you feel, a way to honour them, and a close.
New to ritual as a practice? Read
how rituals work
first.
What you will need
A candle. Something of theirs if you have it, or a photograph. Twenty minutes of quiet.
The practice
Prepare
Sit somewhere quiet. Place the object or photograph in front of you. Light the candle. This candle is for them: it marks the day. Take one slow breath before you begin.
Release (repeat x3)
I am ready to release the weight of carrying this date alone. I release it now. I release it now. I release it now.
Allow (repeat x3)
I am ready to allow this day to be what it is: a day I remember them. I allow it now. I allow it now. I allow it now.
Integrate

Sit with the candle for a minute. Say one thing out loud that you remember about them. It can be anything: a specific detail, a habit they had, something they said. You do not have to choose the most important thing. The ordinary things are also worth naming.
Ground it
The love does not end with the person.
Say it or write it down before you blow the candle out. Carry it with you through the rest of the day.
Where this practice comes from

Original to homemade.education. Candle-lighting as a ritual of remembrance is found across many cultural and religious traditions. The five-part ritual shape (prepare, release, allow, integrate, ground) draws on the ritual structure developed by Rebecca J Page in The Money Journal (2025), adapted here for anniversary grief.
